questions.question: How do I lead without authority?
questions.answer: Lead by influence, not position. (1) Build relationships first — people follow people they trust. (2) Share knowledge generously — become the person others come to for help. (3) Take initiative — volunteer for challenges others avoid. (4) Give credit publicly, take blame privately. (5) Be consistent — reliability builds trust over time.

questions.question: How do I handle a team member who resists my leadership?
questions.answer: First, understand why. Ask them: 'I've noticed some tension. Can you help me understand what's going on?' Listen without defending. Common reasons: they wanted the role, they don't respect your approach, or they're going through personal issues. Address the root cause. If it persists, have a direct conversation about expectations and consequences.

questions.question: What's the most important quality for a leader?
questions.answer: Self-awareness. Leaders who know their strengths, weaknesses, and impact on others make better decisions, build stronger teams, and adapt to challenges. The best leaders surround themselves with people who complement their weaknesses and create environments where truth can be spoken.

questions.question: How do I give feedback without demotivating my team?
questions.answer: Be specific and behavior-focused. Instead of 'Your presentation was bad,' try: 'The data was strong, but the conclusion wasn't clear. What if you added a summary slide?' Also, ask for their perspective first: 'How do you think that went?' This makes feedback a conversation, not a judgment.

questions.question: How do I balance being liked vs being effective?
questions.answer: You don't have to choose — but effectiveness must come first. People respect leaders who are fair, honest, and consistent, even when they make unpopular decisions. Be kind but clear. Set high standards and hold everyone accountable, including yourself. The best leaders are respected more than they're liked.
